audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed as Job's continued discourse: oath by the living Divine, refusal to surrender integrity, rejection of false speech, and description of the godless person's fragile hope and wealth. Preserves tension: Job rejects friends' accusations while still affirming that godlessness has no final security.

verses.restored: Job again took up his discourse and said:
As the Divine lives, who has removed my justice, and Shaddai, who has made my soul bitter,
as long as my breath is still in me, and the breath of the Divine is in my nostrils,
my lips will not speak injustice, and my tongue will not utter deceit.
Far be it from me to say you are right; until I expire, I will not remove my integrity from me.
I hold fast my righteousness and will not let it go; my heart does not reproach any of my days.
Let my enemy be as the wicked, and the one rising against me as the unjust.
For what is the hope of the godless when he is cut off, when the Divine draws out his soul?
Will the Divine hear his cry when distress comes upon him?
Will he delight himself in Shaddai? Will he call upon the Divine at all times?
I will teach you about the hand of the Divine; what is with Shaddai I will not hide.
Look, all of you have seen it; why then have you become altogether empty?
This is the portion of a wicked person with the Divine, and the inheritance ruthless ones receive from Shaddai:
If his children multiply, it is for the sword, and his offspring will not be satisfied with bread.
Those who remain of him will be buried by death, and his widows will not weep.
Though he piles up silver like dust and prepares clothing like clay,
he prepares it, but the righteous will wear it, and the innocent will divide the silver.
He builds his house like a moth, like a booth a watchman makes.
Rich he lies down, but it will not be gathered; he opens his eyes, and it is not there.
Terrors overtake him like waters; a storm steals him away in the night.
The east wind lifts him, and he goes; it sweeps him from his place.
It hurls at him and does not spare; from its hand he surely flees.
It claps its hands over him and hisses him from his place.
